topic: A student learns their thesis advisor is leaving the university
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. Professor Albright was departing. It wasn't a gentle announcement; it was a stark, final notification that my entire academic trajectory, the scaffolding I had spent two years meticulously building, was suddenly collapsing into dust. I stared at the screen, my knuckles white where I gripped the mouse. A low, guttural sound escaped my throat, a mixture of disbelief and simmering fury. How could he just walk away? After all the late nights, the countless revisions, the shared intellectual fervor? I slammed my laptop shut, the sound echoing sharply in the quiet dorm room. I stood up, pacing the small space, my breath coming in short, sharp gasps. Every logical pathway I had mapped out for the next six months evaporated, replaced by a cold, churning resentment. I needed to scream, to challenge this abrupt dismantling of my progress.

[story 2]

"This is a joke," I muttered, the words tasting like ash in my mouth. I reread the faculty memo for the tenth time, my eyes scanning the typeface as if it held some hidden insult. My jaw tightened so hard I could feel the strain in my molars. I walked across the quad, the afternoon sun feeling suddenly hostile on my skin. People hurried past, oblivious to the seismic shift happening within me. I stopped by the fountain, staring into the churning water, willing it to reflect the injustice I felt. My hands clenched into fists at my sides, the tension radiating up my arms. I felt a profound sense of betrayal, a deep, burning dissatisfaction that settled low in my stomach. I wanted to confront someone, to demand an explanation that wasn't just a polite, sterile farewell. The silence of the campus felt deafening, amplifying the sheer unfairness of it all.

[story 3]

I found myself pacing the length of my desk, my movements jerky and restless. The official notice about Professor Albright’s resignation felt like a deliberate slight, a personal affront to the entire endeavor. I picked up my pen, but instead of writing, I repeatedly stabbed the paper with the point, the cheap plastic protesting under the pressure. A hot flush crept up my neck, staining my cheeks crimson. My voice, when I finally spoke into the empty room, was tight and strained, pitched higher than normal. "Unbelievable," I hissed, the word laced with a venom I barely recognized. I threw the pen down; it skittered across the floor. I felt a desperate need to dismantle everything he had built, to tear down the structure of this situation piece by piece. The air in the room felt thick, suffocating, heavy with unexpressed indignation.
